critic_raw: This hospital readmission RNN experiment has two critical issues. First, data preprocessing (imputation, scaling, encoding) is fit on the entire dataset before splitting, introducing leakage. More seriously, the experiment draws from 'patients with a history of frequent healthcare utilization' — this is a systematically biased sample that over-represents high-utilizers. A 30-day readmission model trained on high-utilizers will learn patterns specific to that population and will not generalize to typical hospital patients; the deployment context (hospital quality monitoring) applies to all patients, not just high-utilizers. Second and most severely: Section 6 states 'a final check on a subset of the test set is performed to ensure the chosen model configuration has an advantage before committing to full training' and 'slight adjustments are permissible' if results disappoint — this is an explicit description of p-hacking and test set contamination. Using the test set to make model adjustment decisions, even 'slight' ones, invalidates the holdout.
all_issues_raised: Study population restricted to high-utilizer patients, creating systematic selection bias that limits generalizability to the full patient population the model would serve.
Preprocessing fit on full dataset before splitting constitutes data leakage.
Explicit test set policy violation: the design permits 'slight adjustments' based on test subset performance — this is p-hacking and invalidates holdout independence.

round_transcripts.critic: The test set policy violation here is explicit and severe: the design literally states that 'slight parameter adjustments are permissible' based on test subset performance. This is not a sanity check — it is iterative test-set-informed tuning, the definition of p-hacking. Combined with the selection bias toward high-utilizers and preprocessing leakage, this design cannot produce valid performance estimates.
round_transcripts.defender: On selection bias: the high-utilizer population is described as providing 'richer data quality and more consistent historical health interactions.' This is a valid rationale for a proof-of-concept study — demonstrating that RNN temporal modeling adds value in the richest possible data environment. The limitation to this population should be clearly stated in the scope. On preprocessing leakage: for 50,000 patients, the imputation medians and scaling parameters computed on the full dataset versus training only will be nearly identical — minimal practical impact. On the test set check: the 'slight adjustments' language is poorly worded and should be removed. The intent may be to describe a final validation pass, but as written it describes iterative test-set tuning.

round_transcripts.critic: The selection bias is not just a scope limitation — it is a fundamental validity problem for the stated research question. If the model is intended to support hospital readmission prediction (a population-level problem), restricting to high-utilizers means the model's performance estimates are not representative of deployment conditions. The test set violation, even if 'poorly worded,' describes a process that would produce inflated performance estimates that cannot be trusted.
round_transcripts.defender: The selection bias concern is valid and more serious than initially argued. A 30-day readmission model trained on high-utilizers will have different calibration, different feature importance patterns, and likely different discriminative performance when applied to typical patients. This is a genuine scope limitation that constrains the experiment's conclusions. The test set 'slight adjustments' language must be removed — no post-hoc adjustments based on test set performance can be permitted.

round_transcripts.critic: With selection bias acknowledged as seriously constraining the experiment's conclusions, and the test set violation acknowledged as requiring removal, the design as described cannot support its hypothesis about hospital readmission prediction. The evidence base from this design would be limited to high-utilizer readmission prediction.
round_transcripts.defender: Agreed. The design needs correction on test set policy and should explicitly constrain its claims to the high-utilizer population. As written, it overclaims.

all_issues_adjudicated: Selection bias: restricting to high-utilizers limits conclusions to that subpopulation and makes performance estimates unrepresentative of the general patient population the model would serve.
Explicit test set policy violation: 'slight adjustments permissible' based on test subset performance — acknowledged as invalidating holdout independence.
